title: Translating Subgraphs to Nodes Makes Simple GNNs Strong and Efficient for Subgraph Representation Learning
abstract: Subgraph representation learning has emerged as an important problem, but it is by default approached with specialized graph neural networks on a large global graph. These models demand extensive memory and computational resources but challenge modeling hierarchical structures of subgraphs. In this paper, we propose Subgraph- To-Node (S2N) translation, a novel formulation for learning representations of subgraphs. Specifically, given a set of subgraphs in the global graph, we construct a new graph by coarsely transforming subgraphs into nodes. Demonstrating both theoretical and empirical evidence, S2N not only significantly reduces memory and computational costs compared to state-of-the-art models but also outperforms them by capturing both local and global structures of the subgraph. By leveraging graph coarsening methods, our method outperforms baselines even in a data-scarce setting with insufficient subgraphs. Our experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate that fined-tuned models with S2N translation can process 183 - 711 times more subgraph samples than state-of-the-art models at a better or similar performance level.
impact: This paper aims to advance the field of graph representation learning, and it is difficult to expect direct societal consequences. Instead, we discuss a potential concern about bias and fairness that has not been confirmed in theory or practice. Models with S2N pass messages between all samples within the batch; thus, the majority in a batch can be overrepresented during prediction and the bias in representations can be amplified. The tasks covered in this paper are unrelated to this, but practitioners should be aware of this for tasks that contain sensitive attributes.

query_input.scenario: Memory-efficient GNN deployment on edge devices
query_input.issue: Aggressive coarsening to meet memory constraints can introduce representation artifacts
hazard: Coarsening subgraphs into node representations discards fine-grained graph connectivity, potentially introducing embedding inaccuracies.
exposure: Users of edge-based graph inference applications (e.g., mobile recommender systems)

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Users depend on accurate graph-based recommendations without the ability to detect or mitigate embedding errors from coarsening.
hipporag_result.impact: Users experience degraded recommendation quality, leading to poor decision-making and reduced trust in the application.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Coarsening subgraphs into node representations discards fine-grained connectivity, introducing embedding inaccuracies that, through edge-based inference applications, trigger users' dependence on accurate recommendations without error detection, leading to degraded recommendation quality, poor decisions, and reduced trust.

dose_response: Coarsening subgraphs into node representations discards fine-grained connectivity, introducing artifacts that in memory-efficient GNNs on edge devices cause degraded recommendations and potential data leakage when resource-limited devices lack verification.
vulnerability: Resource-limited edge devices with no fallback verification for embedding quality
impact: Degraded recommendations cause user frustration and potential leakage of sensitive data through mis-grouping
